## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
41	  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
42	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
43	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
44	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
45	  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")
46	
47	## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)
48	
49	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
50	is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
51	**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
52	Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
53	verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)
54	
55	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
56	
57	The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
58	asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:
59	
60	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
61	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
62	  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.
63	
64	Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
65	revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
66	auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
67	protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
68	by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
69	taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
70	memo.)
71	
72	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
73	
74	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
75	
76	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
77	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
78	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
79	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
80	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
81	  governor buys nothing.
82	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
83	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
84	  are parameters).
85	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
86	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
87	
88	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
89	
90	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
91	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
92	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
93	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
94	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
95	
96	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
97	
98	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
99	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
100	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
101	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
102	
103	## What done looks like
104	
105	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
106	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
107	collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
108	verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
109	throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).
110	
111	The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
112	calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
113	vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
114	deferred — recorded here as shape.
115	
116	## Cinched decisions
117	
118	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
119	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
120	  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
121	  first-class.
122	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
123	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
124	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
125	- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
126	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
127	- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
128	  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
129	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
130	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
131	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
132	  not in this heat.
133	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
134	  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
135	  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
136	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
137	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
138	
139	## Retired cinches
140	
141	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
142	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
143	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
144	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
145	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
146	
147	## Open — resolve within the heat
148	
149	- Where the declared ledger physically lives (a separate access-restricted object vs a GAR
150	  artifact) — explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director holds objectCreator there); the
151	  write-ACL must be held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant; see The audit).
152	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
153	  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
154	- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
155	  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
156	- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
157	  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).
158	
159	## Sources
160	
161	rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
162	split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
163	shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
164	(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
165	points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
166	RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
167	passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent).

# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
else.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.

Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
  governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin

  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult

throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +

## Open — resolve within the heat

- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);

content.text:
"Pale" isn't a citizen-model coinage — it's a defined concept from your CMK Rules of Engagement (`Tools/cmk/claude-cmk-roe.md`, the section "The ungovernable, and the Pale"). The citizen paddock just *invokes* it. So it's load-bearing because it points at something already cinched elsewhere.

The plain meaning: **the Pale is the edge of your realm of control** — the fence line between what you can fix and what you can only cope with. The word is literal — a "pale" is a fence stake (palisade), and historically "the Pale" was the boundary of a jurisdiction; "beyond the pale" = outside where your law reaches. That's exactly the sense here.

The recognition test that defines it (verbatim shape from the ROE):

> **Can I edit the source of the failure?**
> - **Yes** → it's ours. Full sovereignty: fix it, mandate one canonical form, tolerate nothing.
> - **No** → it's at the Pale. No interior discipline reaches it. You can't legislate a neighbor; you can only conduct yourself well.

Inside the Pale you *legislate* (mandate canonical forms, crash on violation — Interface Contamination Discipline). At the Pale you can't legislate, so you *conduct*: characterize the foreign behavior precisely, contain it at one membrane, absorb only the surveyed signature, log the bend, and retire the membrane when the neighbor heals.

**Why it's load-bearing in *this* paddock specifically** — it's the justification for the asymmetric healing:

- A **deficit** (IAM short of the declared ledger) means *our own grant* under-delivered. The source of the failure is our code — inside the Pale — so we auto-converge.
- A **surplus** (IAM beyond the ledger) means someone touched IAM *outside our verbs* — a human at the console, another system. We can't edit that actor; it crossed the Pale. So the audit never auto-revokes — it reports, and a human adjudicates.

So "the asymmetry *is* the Pale recognition test" is saying: which way we heal is decided entirely by which side of the fence the cause sits on. That's the whole reason the two directions get treated differently rather than both auto-correcting.

This also connects to the thread I flagged earlier — when a federate's STS exchange creates IAM state, the question becomes *which side of the Pale that sits on*, and the answer may not be the same as a human at the console. Worth pinning down when you get to federation.

## The edit — not simple, so I stopped

It's not a typo-grade change. "Pale" has set as a **load-bearing concept across your whole CMK corpus**, not just its definition site:

- **Definition site:** `claude-cmk-roe.md` (4 uses)
- **This heat's paddock** + the citizen memo (`memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`)
- **~7 memos** using it as a class/status/framing label ("Pale-membrane status", "Pale conduct", "Pale framing", `Class: Pale`)
- **rbk source + specs:** `rbgo_OAuth.sh`, `rbgc_Constants.sh`, `RBS0-SpecTop.adoc`, `RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc`, `CBG-CloudBuildGuide.md`

So ~14 files plus the heat paddock (which is JJK state — a different edit gesture than a file write). Renaming the definition site alone would *fracture* the term: the ROE would say "Palisade" while every memo still says "Pale." It's all-or-nothing, and "all" is a deliberate vocabulary migration, not a quick fix — which trips your own condition ("*if* it is a simple edit"). So I held off.

Two flags before you decide:

1. **Spelling:** standard English is **"Palisade"** (one L); you wrote "Pallisade." I'd use the one-L form unless you want the archaic double-L on purpose.
2. **Connotation shift:** "Pale" was almost certainly chosen for *"beyond the pale"* — a **jurisdictional boundary**, the edge of where your law reaches. "Palisade" is a **defensive wall**. Both fit "realm's edge," but the meaning drifts from *edge-of-jurisdiction* (you can't legislate past it) to *fortification* (you defend behind it). For the recognition test ("can I edit the source?"), the jurisdiction sense is the more exact one. Your call whether the gain in evocativeness is worth the slight semantic drift.

Say the word and I'll do the full migration in one sweep (files + paddock). I'd just want to do it all at once.

---

## AWS Bedrock — can it cathect to this model?

Short version: **yes to the governance, no to the GCP bindings — and the line between those two is already drawn in your model.** You do *not* need a parallel set of roles. Here's the reframe I'd offer.

**AWS isn't a third tier — it's a different axis.** The paddock's tier-blindness abstracts over *how you authenticate* (citizen = keyfile/signed-JWT mint; federate = IdP/STS mint) while the **enforcement plane stays GCP IAM**. AWS varies a *different* thing: the enforcement plane itself (AWS IAM), and what a grant even *means*. So tier-blindness doesn't absorb AWS for free — but it's the wrong tool for the right reason.

**What ports cleanly (substrate-agnostic governance):** the decoupling of identity-lifecycle from capability-lifecycle; named capability-sets; the declared ledger; the asymmetric audit (deficit auto-converge / surplus report); the dissolved verb vocabulary; the Palisade test. None of that is GCP-flavored — it's pure governance architecture.

**What needs a new adapter (substrate-specific):** what a capability-set *member is* (a GCP IAM binding vs an AWS policy/role attachment), the token mint, the audit's enforcement query, the identity primitive (GCP SA vs AWS role). This is the part that *can't* be plane-blind.

So your worry — "a whole parallel set of roles" — resolves to: you write parallel capability-set **definitions** (AWS grant bundles) that live in the **same** ledger, audit, and verbs. Your governor/director/retriever notions survive as AWS-flavored capability-sets, not a separate regime.

**The part you'll like — federation *is* the cross-cloud bridge.** You said you get the idea of federation but not the details. Here's the detail that matters for you: the same STS token-exchange seam the paddock plans for "external IdP → GCP" is *mechanically the same machinery* that lets one cloud's identity assume another cloud's role (a GCP identity → OIDC token → AWS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` → AWS creds). So the paddock's "single code seam" — one identity-keyed token accessor — **pays off twice**: once for the federation tier, once as the hook that could let an *existing citizen* reach into Bedrock without a parallel keyfile regime. Building it tier-blind now isn't only federation insurance; it's also the AWS reach-across hook.

**Counsel for *this* heat:** don't pull AWS in — it's out of scope and the MVP discipline is right. The cheap insurance is purely conceptual: keep the capability-set *interface* from welding "GCP IAM binding" into its shape, even though GCP is its only implementation today. That's the exact same bet the paddock already makes (tier-blind interface, one implementation) — just extended to "plane" as a latent second axis. Name the axis in the shape; build only GCP. Resist actually building a plane-parameter now — that'd be the premature generalization your load-bearing-complexity principle warns against.

Where I'm confident vs guessing, so you can weight it: I'm confident about the **two-axis framing** and that the **architecture ports while the bindings don't** — that falls straight out of your own model. I'm *reasoning, not certain*, about the exact AWS mechanism fit (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity / OIDC) and how your Depot maps onto Bedrock, because I don't know your Bedrock plan's details yet.

content.text:
Good — let me give it a real first think-through. I'll lead with the headline and then show the reasoning, because the model itself sharpens your "probably not" into something more precise than instinct.

**Headline: your "probably not" is right, and the reason is that you're conflating three layers the citizen model already pulls apart.** The Depot is the wrong home — but not because Bedrock doesn't belong in *a* home. It's because "Depot" is one specific layer, and Bedrock-roles and Bedrock-the-resource live in two *different* layers.

**The three layers (the core tool for this whole question):**

1. **Governance** — citizen / capability-set / declared ledger / audit. Substrate-agnostic. Bedrock shares this wholesale.
2. **Enforcement plane** — what enforces a grant and what it means (GCP IAM vs AWS IAM). Bedrock needs its own adapter (last turn's point).
3. **Resource aggregate** — the named, billed, access-bounded thing capabilities are *scoped to*. **A Depot is this layer** — specifically a GCP project holding the image supply chain (GAR, Cloud Build, hallmarks).

Once you see those three, the Depot question dissolves: **a Depot is a layer-3 resource aggregate, and it's domain-specific** — it's the *image-supply-chain* home (its holdings are arks/hoards/bullions; its roles build and retrieve trusted images). A Bedrock endpoint isn't a holding of that type, and forcing it in would make the Depot either span two clouds (breaking its identity as a GCP project) or absorb a category error. So Bedrock wants a **sibling** resource aggregate, not a slot inside the Depot. (And it'd earn its own minted name — overloading "Depot" to mean "any managed cloud aggregate" would dilute a precise concept into a vague bucket; that's the over-abstraction your load-bearing principle warns against.)

**Here's the part that I think actually answers your worry — the model makes "Depot as home for roles" obsolete even for GCP.** Under the citizen model, the management home for roles is the **declared ledger**, not the Depot. Citizen↔capability-set intent lives in the ledger; the Depot becomes just a resource the capability-sets *point at*. So "should the Depot be the management home for Bedrock roles" is already answered for *Depot's own* roles: it isn't the home — the ledger is. Bedrock extends to "new capability-sets in the same ledger, pointing at a new aggregate." That's it. No parallel governance regime.

**On "roles like but distinct from Retriever and Director" — you've named the shape exactly.** There's a real structural rhyme: Director is the privileged *constructor* (builds trusted artifacts), Retriever the low-priv *consumer* (pulls and uses), split for blast-radius. Bedrock wants the same split — a **Provisioner** (sets up the protected space: model access, provisioned throughput, guardrails, VPC endpoints, audit logging) and an **Invoker** (just calls the model at runtime). The *pattern* (split construct from consume) ports; the *contents* (Cloud Build config vs Bedrock config) don't. That gap between pattern-ports and contents-don't is precisely what the capability-set abstraction exists to hold. So: distinct capability-sets, related by analogy, unified by the shared ledger/audit/verbs. The model is *built for exactly this*.

**The non-obvious one — your legal-isolation motive makes the audit load-bearing, not just convenient.** If Bedrock's value is a *legally sound protected space* (private details never reaching the vendor — this is the same confidentiality boundary as your APCK PHI work), then the claim "this space is protected" is only as strong as your ability to **prove who could touch it**. The declared-ledger-vs-IAM audit *is* that proof — and the asymmetric healing fits a compliance regime perfectly: a **surplus** (someone got invoke-access outside your verbs) is exactly the unauthorized-access event a protected-space regime must detect and escalate to a human. So the citizen model's audit isn't ops hygiene here; it's arguably part of the legal substrate.

**Why this all matters for the heat you're actually building:** federation and the AWS extension *compose*. The full future picture is a **federate** (external-IdP-backed user) holding a **capability-set** that grants *invoke* on a private Bedrock model, enforced by **AWS IAM** reached via cross-cloud STS, audited against the **declared ledger**. Each layer does one job and they stack. That composition is the real reason to get the citizen model's seams clean now — and it's confirmation you're building the right thing.

---

Where I'm guessing, so you can weight it: I'm confident about the three-layer decomposition and the "Depot is layer-3, ledger is the role-home" conclusions — those fall straight out of your own model. I'm reasoning from general AWS knowledge on the Bedrock specifics.

And the one unknown that would most change the answer, worth your steer: **is this single-tenant or multi-tenant isolation?** I.e., one private model for your org, versus each federated user/org getting an *isolated* inference space. If multi-tenant, the resource aggregate may be *per-federate*, and isolation-mapping becomes the dominant design question — a much bigger fork than anything above. content.text:
You're circling something real, and I think the center of it is move #4 — so let me lead there, because once the ledger reifies, the other three fall into place around it.

**The ledger was never just a mechanism — it's the polity, and reifying it is right *because federation needs something to federate into.*** Here's the tell: you said "a small corporation's *collection* of depots and bedrock instances." A collection needs a container, and that container is exactly the thing an external user federates *into*. Federation protocols (OIDC, SAML, Workforce pools) are all fundamentally "admit this external identity into THIS administrative domain." So the home isn't a nicety you're adding — it's the **trust boundary federation requires**, and you were going to need it the moment you committed to making federation easy. Your instinct found it from the other side.

**Its job — and its hard limit.** The home is the **federation boundary + the cross-aggregate citizen registry**: where citizens are admitted, where capability-sets are defined, the seat that makes one citizen coherent across depots, bedrock instances, GCP, AWS, citizen-tier and federate-tier. What it is **not**: an enforcement plane. The home *declares* intent, IAM *enforces*, the audit *reconciles* — keep those three verbs distinct or the "home" metaphor will tempt someone into thinking it gatekeeps. It doesn't; it's the book of record and the door federation opens onto. (And: this is *shape to record*, not MVP build. The heat still builds the ledger-as-mechanism. Naming the home is cheap vocabulary that sharpens the federation framing; building "home administration" is a later heat.)

**This is also why Depot gets legal-soundness for free.** Legal-soundness isn't a feature of an aggregate — it's a property of the governance layer, and the home *is* that layer. Specifically the **provability spine**: declared-ledger-vs-IAM audit = "prove exactly who could touch any aggregate, detect anyone who shouldn't." That spine ports to Depot, Bedrock, anything — build it once. One precision so you don't over-trust it: the spine gives you *access-provability* uniformly; it does **not** give you *data-isolation guarantees* (vendor-won't-train-on-your-data) — that's plane-and-contract-specific (Bedrock's terms, GCP's DPA) and differs per aggregate by what it holds. So "legal soundness for Depot too" is partly free (provability) and partly its own per-aggregate property (what confidentiality even means for images vs prompts). Two knobs, not one.

**The image conduit — and a refinement to "entirely distinct primitives."** Your firm constraint is right and it's actually a *strong* posture, not a compromise: no image builds in AWS, one build authority (Director, in GCP), vouched images conveyed into the Bedrock universe. But notice what that means for roles — the model access *is* entirely distinct (a new Invoke/Provision family), but the **image-pull side is not new at all: it's the Retriever, reaching across clouds.** "Summon a vouched image locally" with "locally" = your AWS compute. So the Bedrock universe has two role-families: genuinely-new Bedrock primitives, *plus* the Retriever pattern ported to a new plane. One caveat that's load-bearing: **the conduit has to carry the vouch *verification*, not just the bytes** — if vetted images land in AWS but nothing there can check the vouch, you've moved trusted code into a place that can't prove it's trusted. Do that, and you get a quietly beautiful result: your protected inference space runs *only provenance-locked code*. Not just "who can invoke the model is audited" but "every container touching the protected data traces to your Depot." That's the legal story tightening another notch.

**On the cult nouns — concept first, and there's a collision to resolve before any mint.** The home wants a civic-register word (to rhyme with *citizen*): seeds like **Civitas** (the body of citizens — etymological rhyme) or **Demesne** (the feudal domain). 18	
19	## Bash Utility Kit (BUK) Concepts
20	
21	BUK provides tabtarget/launcher infrastructure for bash-based tooling.
22	
23	### TabTarget System
24	
25	TabTargets are lightweight shell scripts in `tt/` that serve as the CLI entry point for all operations. They delegate to workbenches via launchers — no business logic lives in tabtargets.
26	
27	**Discoverability**: `ls tt/` shows all available commands. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-<TAB>`.
28	
29	**Naming pattern**: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
30	
31	| Part | Purpose | Example |
32	|------|---------|---------|
33	| **Colophon** | Routing identifier (includes hyphen, workbench matches on this) | `rbw-cr`, `buw-tt-ll` |
34	| **Frontispiece** | Human-readable description (PascalCase) | `Rack` |
35	| **Imprint** | Optional target parameter (nameplate moniker, fixture name, etc.) | `tadmor` |
36	
37	- The `.` is the delimiter between parts
38	- The hyphen is part of the colophon (not a separator)
39	
40	Example: `tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — colophon `rbw-cr` routes to the crucible rack command, frontispiece tells you what it does, imprint `tadmor` selects the nameplate.
41	
42	Multiple tabtargets can share the same colophon but differ by imprint:
43	```
44	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
45	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.srjcl.sh
46	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.pluml.sh
47	```
48	
49	### BUK Vocabulary
50	
51	| Term | Definition |
52	|------|------------|
53	| **Zipper** | BCG-compliant module kindling colophon→module→command array constants |
54	| **Workbench** | Routes commands: `{prefix}w_workbench.sh` |
55	| **Testbench** | Routes tests: `{prefix}t_testbench.sh` |
56	| **Folio** | Runtime target value (`BUZ_FOLIO`) passed to a command — nameplate moniker, role name, etc. How it arrives depends on the channel |
57	| **Channel** | Enrollment-time declaration of how a colophon receives its folio: `imprint` (from filename — one tabtarget per target), `param1` (command-line argument — single tabtarget), or empty (no folio needed) |
58	
59	**Key files:**
60	- `Tools/buk/buc_command.sh` — command utilities
61	- `Tools/buk/bud_dispatch.sh` — dispatch utilities
62	- `Tools/buk/buw_workbench.sh` — workbench
63	
64	Full spec: `Tools/buk/README.md`
65	
66	## Forbidden Shell Operations
67	
68	**Never use `cd` in Bash commands — NO exceptions.**
69	
70	The working directory persists between Bash tool calls. A single `cd` corrupts ALL subsequent commands that use relative paths, including every `./tt/` tabtarget.
71	
72	- Use absolute paths instead of cd'ing
73	
74	**There is no safe cd.** Do not reason that "I'll cd back" — the next tool call may be yours or another Claude Code session's, and it will break.
75	
76	## Tool Git Discipline
77	
78	Tools never commit in the consumer's codebase. A tool MAY presume git and refuse downstream steps on a dirty tree, but it never stages or commits — the operator commits with their usual workflow. The uniform gate is `bug_require_clean_tree "<operation>"` (BUG module); a verb that installs into tracked config calls it first, so an install-then-forgot-to-commit cannot silently ride into a later build.
79	
80	## TabTarget Invocation Discipline
81	
82	**Never wrap tabtarget invocations with `tee`, `tail`, `head`, `grep`, `2>&1`, or any other pipe — NO exceptions.**
83	
84	Tabtargets self-log to `../logs-buk/`:
85	- `last.txt` — most recent invocation across all tabtargets
86	- `same-{cmd}.txt` — most recent invocation of this specific tabtarget
87	- `hist-{cmd}-{timestamp}.txt` — historical invocations
88	
89	Both stdout and stderr are captured. Adding your own `tee` or `2>&1` duplicates work the tool already did. The real hazard is piping a tabtarget into `tail`/`head`/`grep`: zsh defaults `pipefail` OFF, so the pipeline returns the last command's exit status — usually 0. **A failing test reports as success.**
90	
91	- Run the tabtarget directly, then read from `../logs-buk/` in a separate command
92	- Environment variables before the command are fine: `BURE_CONFIRM=skip ./tt/foo.sh`
93	- If you genuinely must pipe live output (rare — usually you can read the log instead), set `-o pipefail` on the same line, or check `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` (bash) / `${pipestatus[1]}` (zsh)
94	
95	```
96	# Wrong — exit code is from `tail`, not the tabtarget; failures masked
97	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh 2>&1 | tee /tmp/log | tail -80
98	
99	# Wrong — even a bare `| head` discards the real signal
100	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh | head -50
101	
102	# Right — separate commands; exit code preserved
103	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh
104	tail -80 ../logs-buk/last.txt
105	```
106	
107	**There is no safe `tee | tail`.** Do not reason "I'll just truncate the output for readability" — the truncation and the exit-code-eating are inseparable. Truncate by reading the log file afterward.
108	
109	## Test Execution Discipline
110	
111	Run test fixture tabtargets **sequentially, never in parallel**. Test fixtures share regime state and container/network namespaces — parallel execution causes resource conflicts and false failures.
112	
113	```
114	# Correct: run one at a time
115	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh
116	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh
117	
118	# Wrong: never run fixtures concurrently
119	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh & tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh &
120	```

1	# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-27
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
8	key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
9	roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
10	For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:
11	
12	1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
13	   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
14	   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
15	   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.
16	
17	2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
18	   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
19	   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
20	   person's access" button.
21	
22	The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
23	why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"
24	
25	## Approaches Evaluated
26	
27	### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)
28	
29	Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
30	regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
31	directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.
32	
33	- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
34	  nothing Google-specific installed.
35	- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.
36	
37	### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation
38	
39	User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
40	`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
41	`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
42	`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.
43	
44	- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
45	  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
46	- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
47	  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
48	  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
49	  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.
50	
51	Verdict: dismissed.
52	
53	### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)
54	
55	Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
56	"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
57	once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
58	Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
59	refresh token, then call
60	
61	```
62	POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
63	Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
64	```
65	
66	to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
67	`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.
68	
69	- **Pros:**
70	  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
71	  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
72	    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
73	  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
74	    binding (server-side state).
75	  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
76	    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
77	  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
78	    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
79	    model.
80	- **Cons:**
81	  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
82	    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
83	  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
84	    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
85	  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
86	    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.
87	
88	## Mechanism Detail
89	
90	### Authentication flow
91	
92	1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
93	   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
94	   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
95	   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
96	   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
97	   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
98	   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
99	   cached locally.
100	2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
101	   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
102	   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
103	   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
104	   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
105	3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
106	   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
107	   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
108	   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
109	   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
110	   the API call.
111	
112	### Storage shape
113	
114	Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:
115	
116	| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
117	|---|---|---|
118	| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
119	| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |
120	
121	Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):
122	
123	- User access token (~1 hour)
124	- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)
125	
126	Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
127	permissions, gitignored.
128	
129	### IAM binding model
130	
131	Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
132	act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
133	IAM policy on the SA itself:
134	
135	```
136	Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
137	  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
138	  Member:  user:alice@example.com
139	```
140	
141	Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
142	populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:
143	
144	```
145	                  Depot D1              Depot D2
146	alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
147	bob@...       Director              Retriever
148	carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
149	```
150	
151	Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
152	from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
153	policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
154	policies on the SAs are the registry.
155	
156	### Administrative home
157	
158	Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
159	Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
160	within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
161	rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
162	of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.
163	
164	## Coexistence with RBRA
165	
166	Additive in the near term, not a replacement:
167	
168	- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
169	  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
170	- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
171	- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
172	  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
173	  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
174	- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.
175	
176	Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
177	is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
178	this work.
179	
180	## Threat Model Summary
181	
182	| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
183	|---|---|---|
184	| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
185	| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
186	| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
187	| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
188	| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |
189	
190	The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
191	RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
192	admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.
193	
194	## Open Questions
195	
196	- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
197	  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
198	  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
199	  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
200	- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
201	  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
202	  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
203	  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
204	  screen and ToS.
205	- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
206	  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
207	  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
208	- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
209	  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
210	- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
211	  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
212	  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
213	  loads.
214	
215	## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items
216	
217	No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
218	to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
219	credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
220	mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
221	credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
222	about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
223	browser-minted tokens.
224	
225	## Trigger to Revisit
226	
227	Either:
228	
229	- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
230	- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
231	  than being theoretically frictionful).
232	
233	## Notes on Conversation Origin
234	
235	Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
236	owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
237	"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
238	"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
239	`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
240	live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
241	bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
242	Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
243	auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
244	extension on top, not a new dependency layer.

# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration

Date: 2026-04-27

## Problem

Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:

1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.

2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
   person's access" button.

The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"

## Approaches Evaluated

### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)

Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.

- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
  nothing Google-specific installed.
- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.

### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation

User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.

- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.

Verdict: dismissed.

### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)

Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
refresh token, then call

```
POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
```

to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.

- **Pros:**
  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
    binding (server-side state).
  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
    model.
- **Cons:**
  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.

## Mechanism Detail

### Authentication flow

1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
   cached locally.
2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
   the API call.

### Storage shape

Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:

| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |

Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):

- User access token (~1 hour)
- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)

Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
permissions, gitignored.

### IAM binding model

Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
IAM policy on the SA itself:

```
Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
  Member:  user:alice@example.com
```

Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:

```
                  Depot D1              Depot D2
alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
bob@...       Director              Retriever
carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
```

Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
policies on the SAs are the registry.

### Administrative home

Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.

## Coexistence with RBRA

Additive in the near term, not a replacement:

- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.

Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
this work.

## Threat Model Summary

| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
|---|---|---|
| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |

The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.

## Open Questions

- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
  screen and ToS.
- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
  loads.

## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items

No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
browser-minted tokens.

## Trigger to Revisit

Either:

- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
  than being theoretically frictionful).

## Notes on Conversation Origin

Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
extension on top, not a new dependency layer.

180	Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
181	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
182	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).
183	
184	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
185	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
186	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
187	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
188	
189	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
190	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
191	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
192	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
193	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
194	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
195	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
196	
197	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
198	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
199	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
200	{rbtgi_depot}
201	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
202	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
203	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
204	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
205	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
206	design conversation pending heat.
207	
208	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
209	A
210	{giam_service_account}
211	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
212	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
213	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
215	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
216	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
217	
218	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
219	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
220	A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
221	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
222	bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
223	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
224	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
