The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.

**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.

**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
{rbtgi_depot}
creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
A
{giam_service_account}
in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.

content.text:
Both threads landed somewhere richer than expected. Here's what I found.

## Manor — verdict: appropriate it, you're *promoting* not repurposing

Your recollection is right: **Manor is vocabulary, not machinery.** It's a display constant (`RBYC_MANOR`/`RBYC_MANORS` in `rbyc_common.sh`) used purely in handbook *prose* — `buh_line` calls in the Payor/Governor onboarding wrappers. There's no Manor regime, no Manor object, no lifecycle verb. "Payor establishes a Manor" is a guided human procedure (make a GCP project + OAuth + billing), not code operating on a Manor. Nothing to refactor — appropriating it costs prose + one linked-term gloss.

But it's better than a free slot: its *existing* meaning already leans your way — "an administrative seat" that hosts "the OAuth client and billing," and it's already used as a **container above projects** ("disjoint GCP projects on a shared payor manor" — BUS0, regime comments, even the Rust test docs). That's the cross-aggregate-container semantics you wanted for the home, latent in the current word. The only shift: today Manor ≈ "the GCP project"; to become the cloud-agnostic polity it generalizes to "the seat," and "the GCP project with OAuth+billing" demotes to *the Manor's GCP establishment*. That resolves last turn's generalize-vs-above fork — **generalize-Manor**, on evidence.

## The bigger find: your roadmap already sketched most of the AWS side

This is the thing to know — you've been re-deriving architecture that's **already in RBSHR Horizon**, predating this whole conversation. Three matches to what you've said:

- **"Asymmetric sovereignty"** (a cinched stance): RB governs identities *only* in GCP; AWS-side is "delivered artifacts and policy templates, **not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever**." Building in AWS is called out as the thing that *would* trigger a parallel role hierarchy — so your "I don't want to stand up builds in AWS / no parallel roles" instinct is already the roadmap's law.
- **Your cult-noun cluster already exists, and Bedrock is literally named.** The Horizon floats `embassy / envoy / bullion / entrust / accredit / recall / chantry` ("tentative working language; concepts firmer than words"). **Embassy** = "the AWS consumer's sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot" — an S3 bucket (Object Lock *Compliance mode* — your legal-retention angle) + an IAM role whose trust policy accepts a federated identity + RB-shipped policy shapes the consumer attaches to "EC2 role, ECS task role, **Bedrock execution role**, etc." So Manor = seat; Depot = its GCP holding; **Embassy = its AWS holding** — and an embassy is *sovereign soil inside a foreign power's land*, which is almost too perfect for "a legally-protected private enclave inside AWS."
- **Your image conduit is already mechanized.** **Envoy** = a GCP-side SA (Mason-sibling) whose sole job is cross-cloud push to an embassy; holds *no* long-lived AWS creds — "federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time." **Bullion** = the delivered AWS-side artifact (content-addressed; "may carry a vessel image payload… **model weights**, or any other corpus"; provenance rides as adjacent attestation JSON). That *is* your "conduit of controlled images from depot, no AWS builds."

**Where this conversation genuinely advances it** (so I'm not overselling): the roadmap treats AWS as *delivery premises* — artifacts land, a consumer role reads them. Your new move is **Bedrock as a managed *instance*** with its own access primitives (Invoke/Provision), which is a step past "delivery." Asymmetric sovereignty reconciles it cleanly: those primitives ship as *policy templates the consumer owns*, not RB-owned principals. And the **Manor-as-polity reification** plus the **citizen-model/declared-ledger overlay** are both newer than this roadmap (it predates the citizen model). So: the AWS-delivery skeleton is *there*; your three advances are narrow and additive.

## The federation token-storage answer

Lead, because it corrects the mental model: **nothing manor-minted is stored on the workstation as a durable secret — that's the whole point.** "Manor-generated token" is the load-bearing thing to unlearn. The manor's persistent contribution is *trust anchoring* (it registers the OAuth client) and *authorization* (the IAM binding / declared-ledger capability) — **neither is a token.** The credential is minted by the **IdP** (Google, in your most-developed prior art), and the per-operation token is minted *at the workstation, on demand*.

Your own `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` already worked the storage out (Approach C: REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation). Three tiers:

| Tier | What | Stored how |
|---|---|---|
| Trust config | OAuth client ID/secret (app identity) | At rest, **non-secret**, shipped/installed like RBSPI |
| The one real secret | User **refresh token** | At rest, sensitive but **revocable** (revoke consent / drop binding) — *not* an SA key; 0600, gitignored |
| Per-operation tokens | user access token (~1h) + SA access token (≤1h) | **Ephemeral**, re-minted from the refresh token on expiry — never durable |

So "how is the per-operation token stored": **it isn't, durably** — it's a ≤1-hour cache re-minted on demand. The only persistent sensitive artifact is the revocable refresh token, and **where *it* lives (regime-tree vs `$HOME`) is an explicit open question** in that memo, still unresolved. (AuthN vs authZ, kept clean: the token *proves who*; the IAM binding / ledger capability *says what they may do* — per operation you present a fresh short-lived bearer, and IAM enforces.)

Two sincerity flags on that memo, because it predates the citizen model and two of its conclusions are now **superseded**:
- It says the grant "naturally extends **Governor**." → Under the citizen model that's a *capability-set grant in the declared ledger*, and *authority topology is tier-dependent* — keyfile tier may pull it to Payor, not a standing Governor.
- It says "the policies on the SAs **are** the registry — no central registry needed." → That's exactly the **retired cinch** ("roster derives from IAM" — lossy, makes the audit tautological). The declared ledger replaces it.

The storage answer survives the citizen model; its administrative-home and discovery conclusions don't.

One fork worth naming: your prior art has **two** different "federation" mechanisms — the memo's *Google-OAuth-impersonation* (operator path, well-specified) and the paddock's *external-IdP/STS* language (Workforce pools — less specified). Storage shape is similar; the difference is who holds the residual session (Google vs an external IdP). Your "IdP step" phrasing leans toward the external one — is that what you're picturing, or Google-as-IdP per the memo? That choice changes the at-rest custody story.

---

Given how much of this is already latent in Horizon but pre-citizen-model and pre-Bedrock-as-instance, the high-value move now isn't a fresh capture — it's **reconciling RBSHR's embassy/envoy/bullion cluster with the Manor-polity reification and the citizen/ledger overlay**, so the roadmap reflects where you actually are. Want me to read the rest of that Horizon section and draft that reconciliation — or first settle the Google-vs-external-IdP fork, since it ripples through the storage and the envoy both?

message.content: In short, today's per role RBRA system will be replaced by the 'citizen' model, which does _not_ use IdP but does build out the Manor concept so that my MVP delivery can be written for the 'no organization' model and yet apply when we build out federation.  Is that clear?

content.text:
Yes — clear, and right. That's the paddock's core bet, now with Manor as the named home. Two precisions to keep the edges sharp, both confirmations rather than corrections:

**1. Keyfile stays in the citizen tier — "no IdP" and "still keyfile" are the same fact.** What's replaced is RBRA's *per-role* structure (one key per governor/director/retriever), not the keyfile mechanism itself. The MVP operator still has an SA key on disk — now **one per person**, carrying capability-sets, instead of one per role. The signed-JWT-from-keyfile mint is unchanged. IdP/STS only enters at federation. So you're not removing keyfiles in the MVP; you're re-shaping who holds them and what they mean.

**2. "Build out Manor" = reify it in its *degenerate* form, not build Manor administration.** The MVP gives you exactly one Manor — the no-org operator's own seat — scoping the declared ledger and the citizen(s). What it does **not** build is multi-Manor management or federate-admission machinery (that's the later heat). The Manor is *present but degenerate*: no admission, no IdP, one polity of one. And that's precisely the value — **Manor is the point where IdP later attaches.** Build it now in its IdP-less single-org form, and federation becomes "the Manor learns to admit federates (and maybe multiply)" without reshaping the capability layer or the ledger underneath it.

So your sentence holds: per-role RBRA → citizen model (still keyfile, no IdP), with Manor reified as the single home now so the same delivery generalizes when federation lands.

One consequence worth flagging: **the paddock doesn't currently name Manor** — it calls the home "the declared ledger" (a mechanism). Adopting Manor as the home's name and scope is a genuine addition to the heat's shape. Want me to fold it into the paddock — name the home as the (degenerate, single) Manor, and drop a cross-reference to RBSHR's embassy/envoy/bullion cluster as the federation/AWS horizon it later extends into? I'd update the shape only; the MVP scope stays where it is.

content.text:
Yes — and the part you flagged as the extra step ("even making sure we eliminate any conflicting prior ideas of Manor") is the part I'd endorse *most strongly*. It's not optional aggression; it's what makes the appropriation valid. A Manor that means both "the GCP project with OAuth+billing" (today) and "the cloud-agnostic polity" (your new sense) is polysemous — which breaks the within-domain monosemy your own minting discipline requires. And it's cheap: Manor is pure vocabulary today (a display constant plus handbook prose, no machinery), so the scrub is a gloss-layer pass, not a refactor. Now is also cheaper than later — every turn the old meaning sits, more prose accretes to it.

Why Manor is the *right* single one to advance, not an arbitrary pick: **it's the only term in the cluster the MVP heat actually touches.** Embassy / Envoy / Bullion are AWS-and-federation horizon, and RBSHR already defers them ("tentative working language; trigger: when AWS work moves into a heat"). So the heat selects Manor for you; pulling the rest forward now would drag deferred AWS vocabulary into a keyfile-tier MVP. Advance one — and it's this one.

Two things to make it land cleanly:

**Advance Manor at *container altitude*, not as a flat name.** Define it as the seat/polity, and in the same breath note that Depot is its GCP holding and the AWS holding (Embassy — deferred, don't mint it now) slots in later. You commit one *name* but lock the *shape* it sits at, so the deferred siblings have a clean place to attach. Skip this and you risk re-opening Manor's altitude when Embassy eventually lands.

**Settle the vacancy the scrub creates.** When Manor stops naming "the GCP project hosting OAuth + billing," that establishment role still needs a home — does it fold into Depot, or become a small named sub-concept? Probably small, but it's the concrete residue of the generalization, and it's exactly the generalize-vs-above question from earlier. This is where reading how Manor and Depot actually relate in the code earns its keep.

So: recommend yes — consolidate on Manor now, scrub the old meaning, defer the rest of the cluster. If you want, I'll read the Manor/Depot relationship to settle that vacancy first, then do the consolidation in one pass — scrub the prose/display-constant gloss and reify Manor in the paddock at container altitude, MVP scope unchanged. Want me to start with the Manor/Depot read?

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");

# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Regime Depot - Validator Module

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBRD_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbrd multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBRD_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbrd_*)

zrbrd_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBRD_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbrd already kindled"

  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.

  # Enroll all RBRD variables — single source of truth for validation and rendering

  buv_regime_enroll RBRD

  buv_group_enroll "Depot Identity"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX         2   11  "Prefix for cloud-visible resource names (depot project, GAR repo, GCS bucket, pool stem)"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER        1   26  "Depot moniker — paired with CLOUD_PREFIX to derive depot project ID, GAR repo, pool stem, and bucket"

  buv_group_enroll "GCP Region"
  buv_gname_enroll   RBRD_GCP_REGION           1   32  "GCP region"

  buv_group_enroll "Google Cloud Build Pool"
  buv_gname_enroll   RBRD_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE     3   64  "Machine type for Cloud Build private worker pool (CE format)"

  # Guard against unexpected RBRD_ variables not in enrollment
  buv_scope_sentinel RBRD RBRD_

  # Lock all enrolled RBRD_ variables against mutation
  buv_lock RBRD

  readonly ZRBRD_KINDLED=1
}

zrbrd_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBRD_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbrd not kindled - call zrbrd_kindle first"
}

# Enforce all RBRD enrollment validations and custom format checks
zrbrd_enforce() {
  zrbrd_sentinel

  buv_vet RBRD

  [[ "${RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER}" =~ ^[a-z][a-z0-9]*$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER format: ${RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER} (expected lowercase alphanumeric starting with letter; no hyphens)"

  [[ "${RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX}" =~ ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX format: ${RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX} (expected lowercase starting with letter, ending in hyphen)"

  # Joint-length cap: GCP project IDs max 30 chars. RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID
  # composes as "${RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX}d-${RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER}".
  #
  # Test fixtures (rbtdrp_pristine, rbtdrk_canonical) compose BURS_TINCTURE
  # (1-3 chars) into BOTH the prefix and the moniker before writing rbrd.env
  # for parallel-station disjointness on a shared payor manor. The cap below
  # applies to whatever is in rbrd.env at validation time — production-
  # authored or fixture-written. Fixture bases are short (≤7 chars) so
  # tinctured fixture values stay well under the cap; the joint check exists
  # to catch operator-authored prefix+moniker pairs that would overflow.
  # The validator does not cross-reference BURS_TINCTURE (BURS may not be
  # kindled in every RBRD-enforce context); the literal field values are
  # the contract.
  local -r z_prefix_len=${#RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX}
  local -r z_moniker_len=${#RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER}
  local -r z_joint_len=$(( z_prefix_len + 2 + z_moniker_len ))
  test "${z_joint_len}" -le 30 \
    || buc_die "Joint length exceeds 30-char GCP project ID limit: RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX(${z_prefix_len}) + 'd-'(2) + RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER(${z_moniker_len}) = ${z_joint_len}"
}

######################################################################
# Public Functions (rbrd_*)

# Source an arbitrary RBRD regime file and run the full kindle->enforce
# chain against it, failing on first fault. Test-facing contract surface:
# theurge drives synthetic-malformed regime files through this without
# reaching module internals. Prerequisite: buv kindled.
rbrd_probate() {
  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "rbrd_probate: regime file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "rbrd_probate: cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbrd_kindle
  zrbrd_enforce
}

# eof

  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.

  # Enroll all RBRP variables — single source of truth for validation and rendering

  buv_regime_enroll RBRP

  buv_group_enroll "Payor Project Identity"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID  1  128  "GCP project hosting OAuth client"

  buv_group_enroll "Billing Configuration"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID  0  20  "Billing account for depot projects"

  buv_group_enroll "OAuth Configuration"
  # min=0 deliberate — not every operator has a Payor identity (Retriever-only
  # operators authenticate via JWT SA, never via Payor OAuth). Required-at-use
  # is enforced by test -n in rbgp_Payor.sh consumers. Do not tighten to min=1.
  buv_string_enroll  RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID  0  256  "OAuth 2.0 client identifier"

  buv_group_enroll "Operator Identity"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRP_OPERATOR_EMAIL  0  256  "Operator Google account email for console access"

  # Guard against unexpected RBRP_ variables not in enrollment
  buv_scope_sentinel RBRP RBRP_

  # Lock all enrolled RBRP_ variables against mutation
  buv_lock RBRP

  readonly ZRBRP_KINDLED=1
}

zrbrp_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBRP_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbrp not kindled - call zrbrp_kindle first"
}

# Enforce all RBRP enrollment validations and custom format checks
zrbrp_enforce() {
  zrbrp_sentinel

  buv_vet RBRP

  # Custom format checks beyond buv_ type system
  zrbgc_sentinel
  [[ "${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}" =~ ${RBGC_GLOBAL_PAYOR_REGEX} ]] \
    || buc_die "RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID does not match payor project pattern"

  if test -n "${RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID}"; then
    [[ "${RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID}" =~ ^[A-Z0-9]{6}-[A-Z0-9]{6}-[A-Z0-9]{6}$ ]] \
      || buc_die "RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID must be XXXXXX-XXXXXX-XXXXXX format"
  fi

  if test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}"; then
    [[ "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" =~ \.apps\.googleusercontent\.com$ ]] \
      || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID must end with .apps.googleusercontent.com"
  fi
}

######################################################################
# Public Functions (rbrp_*)

# Source an arbitrary RBRP regime file and run the full kindle->enforce
# chain against it, failing on first fault. Test-facing contract surface:
# theurge drives synthetic-malformed regime files through this without
# reaching module internals. Prerequisites: buv kindled, and RBGC kindled
# (zrbrp_enforce reaches RBGC_GLOBAL_PAYOR_REGEX for the payor-project check).
rbrp_probate() {
  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "rbrp_probate: regime file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "rbrp_probate: cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbrp_kindle
  zrbrp_enforce
}

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBRO_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbro multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBRO_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbro_*)

zrbro_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBRO_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbro already kindled"

  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.

  # Enroll all RBRO variables — single source of truth for validation and rendering
  buv_regime_enroll RBRO

  buv_group_enroll "OAuth Credentials"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET  1  512  "OAuth client secret"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN  1  512  "OAuth refresh token"

  # Guard against unexpected RBRO_ variables not in enrollment
  buv_scope_sentinel RBRO RBRO_

  # Lock all enrolled RBRO_ variables against mutation
  buv_lock RBRO

  readonly ZRBRO_KINDLED=1
}

zrbro_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBRO_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbro not kindled - call zrbro_kindle first"
}

# Enforce all RBRO enrollment validations
zrbro_enforce() {
  zrbro_sentinel
  buv_vet RBRO
}

######################################################################
# Public Functions (rbro_*)

# Load RBRO from RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE (kindle constant from RBRR_SECRETS_DIR)
# Requires: RBRR kindled+enforced+locked (RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE is a lock-step constant)
rbro_load() {
  local z_rbro_file="${RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE}"

  test -f "${z_rbro_file}" || buc_die "RBRO credentials missing (${z_rbro_file}) - run rbgp_payor_install"
  test -r "${z_rbro_file}" || buc_die "RBRO file not readable - check permissions"

  # Source and validate
  source "${z_rbro_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source RBRO credentials"
  zrbro_kindle
  zrbro_enforce
}

# Source an arbitrary RBRO regime file and run the full kindle->enforce
# chain against it, failing on first fault. Test-facing contract surface:
# theurge drives synthetic-malformed regime files through this without
# reaching module internals. Prerequisite: buv kindled.
rbro_probate() {
  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "rbro_probate: regime file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "rbro_probate: cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbro_kindle
  zrbro_enforce
}

content.text:
Both reads land — and the secrets-directory finding answers your storage question more cleanly than expected. Let me give you the token movement first (your stated need), then the firmed Manor opinion.

## IdP token movement — you don't invent the location, it already exists

RBK retains OAuth refresh tokens **today**, in pure bash, in a structured secrets dir. The live picture (`rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh`):

```
${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/          ← RBRR field: "Directory containing credential files"
   governor/   rbra           ← SA keyfile
   retriever/  rbra           ← SA keyfile
   director/   rbra           ← SA keyfile
   payor/      rbro           ← OAuth: client secret + refresh token
   assay/      rbra
```

There's already a **per-account secrets dir, one subdirectory per principal**, holding either a keyfile (RBRA) or an OAuth refresh token (RBRO). So the "live station secrets location" is `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR`, and it already does exactly the dual job.

**The citizen model changes the *key*, not the *mechanism*:** `secrets/{role}/` → `secrets/{citizen}/`. One slot per person; each slot holds their credential. And that *is* your MVP-applies-to-federation seam in storage form — **the layout is tier-blind (a per-citizen slot); only the slot's *contents* differ** (keyfile for citizen tier, refresh token for federation tier). Build the per-citizen layout now holding keyfiles, and federation just changes what fills the slot. It's the storage face of "identity verbs branch by tier, capability verbs are tier-blind."

**The movement itself** (from `rbgp_Payor.sh` / `rbgo_OAuth.sh`):
- **At rest, in the slot:** the refresh token (+ client secret). Long-lived (~6 mo for OAuth) but *revocable* — not an SA key.
- **Never at rest:** the access token. Minted per-operation from the refresh token, held in a shell variable (memory), and explicitly scrubbed from every log (`jq 'del(.access_token, .refresh_token)'`). No access-token-on-disk today.

So the mental-model correction: you don't retain a "manor-generated token." You retain the **refresh token** (IdP-issued, revocable) in the existing secrets slot, and **re-mint a short-lived access token from it per operation**. The Manor's role stays trust-config (the OAuth client in RBRP) + authorization — never minting or holding the operation token.

**Two real decisions** (not "invent a location" — actual forks):
1. **Per-checkout vs `$HOME`-rooted.** `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR` is a *repo*-regime field today — per working tree, dies with `git clean -fdx`. With many citizens and multiplexing across checkouts, `$HOME`-rooted may be right (survives clean, shared). This is the storage fork the April memo already flagged open.
2. **Optional access-token cache.** Today you re-mint per call. If that exchange latency ever bites, caching the ≤1h access token is the one genuinely-new ephemeral category — an optimization, not a requirement.

## Manor/Depot — vacancy was illusory; consolidate cleanly

What "Manor" colloquially names — "the GCP project hosting OAuth + billing" — is **already precisely regime-named**: `RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID` ("GCP project hosting OAuth client") + `RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID` + `RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`, with secrets in RBRO. The build holding is the Depot (RBRD). Manor itself has **no regime** (there's no RBRM) — it's pure prose. So freeing "Manor" to mean the seat orphans nothing:

- **Manor** = the seat/polity (new altitude), containing:
  - **Payor establishment** (RBRP + RBRO) — the OAuth+billing root
  - **Depot** (RBRD) — the GCP build/registry holding
  - **secrets dir** (RBRR) — the Manor's credential store
  - **Embassy** (future, deferred) — the AWS holding
- **Payor** = the founder who *establishes* the Manor — matches the paddock's "payor = founder, outside both."

The scrub is therefore **prose-only**: repoint the `RBYC_MANOR` gloss and the handbook lines saying "the Manor's GCP project hosts OAuth+billing" to attribute that to the Payor establishment, and let Manor name the seat. No machinery, no folding into Depot, no new sub-concept — cleaner than I cautioned last turn.

So my recommendation stands and strengthens: **consolidate on Manor now.** When you're ready I'll do it in one pass — scrub the prose/gloss, and reify Manor in the paddock at container altitude (seat ⊃ Payor-establishment / Depot / secrets-store / future-Embassy), MVP scope unchanged. Proceed — or keep building the model first?

This is the heaviest edit — Manor redefines to the seat; the GCP-project/OAuth/billing attributions move to the Payor establishment (RBRP). |

- **`.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json` (5)** and **`.claude/jjm/jjp_uBuU.md` (1)** — **JJK state.** The Manor paddock reification changes these *through jjx*, never a text edit. If they conflict in your merge, that's a JJK-level resolution, not a hand-merge.

message.content: Well darn, you reminded me what I used to think of as Manor: its just the payor project, that there is one-of.  I'm genuinely uncertain we should have this be infused with AWS administrative powers.  Help me think through this.  The Federation ledger legitimately needs a home... Sorry to re-litigate, I had forgotten.

content.text:
No apology needed — this is the circling working, not backsliding. Nothing's cinched yet, and your gut just caught a real category error in *my* proposal. Let me name it, because once it's named the discomfort resolves cleanly.

**The error was mine: I fused two different altitudes under one word.** "Manor = the one Payor project" is a *holding* — concrete, singular, GCP, OAuth+billing. The federation home/polity is the *container* — abstract, the registry, the trust boundary. Those aren't the same kind of thing, and I'd been quietly promoting Manor to be both. What you're feeling is the altitude collision: a humble GCP billing project being asked to wear cross-cloud administrative powers it has no business holding. Your instinct is right. Manor is a holding.

**The resolution dissolves the AWS-infusion fear without giving up the ledger's home.** Keep Manor exactly as you originally meant it — the Payor project, one-of. Then satisfy "the ledger needs a home" by having **the Manor *host* the ledger**, not by having Manor *become* the polity. And here's the load-bearing distinction: *hosting a book-of-record that records AWS-targeting intent is not administering AWS.* The ledger says "citizen Alice may invoke the embassy's Bedrock model" — recording that intent in a GCS object in the Payor project doesn't give the Payor project one ounce of AWS power. The AWS administrative power lives on the AWS side (the embassy, owned by the consumer, reached by the envoy via federation — exactly RBSHR's asymmetric sovereignty). Manor never touches AWS. It just holds the ledger and keeps billing the depot, like it always did.

**And the leaner claim, which I think is the real prize: you may not need a "polity" concept at all.** The declared ledger *is* the membership roll federation admits into. The Manor hosts it — and the Manor is *already* the natural federation anchor, because it already hosts the OAuth client, so it'd host the workforce pool too. So "what does a federate federate into?" → the Manor's trust config, which is still just the Payor project. One concrete thing, wearing hats it already wears, plus hosting one new object (the ledger). No Civitas, no Demesne, no grand abstraction to mint. That's load-bearing complexity doing its job: don't invent the container when the Payor-project-plus-ledger already covers the need.

It even tightens your existing cinches: the ledger's write-authority is "the grant-authority holder under the active topology," and the keyfile tier "pulls grant authority to the Payor." The Payor project *is* the Manor. So Manor = Payor project = ledger host = ledger-write-authority home. It all lands on one place, and that place is the concrete singleton you always had.

**The one condition that would break the lean model** — so you have the boundary: if you ever go *multi-tenant* (each federated org gets its own isolated polity) or somehow *multi-Manor*, then the polity earns a distinct name above the Manor. As long as Manor is one-of — which is your stated belief — it doesn't. So the rule is: stay lean now; mint a polity only if multi-tenancy becomes real. (That's the same multi-tenant fork I flagged a couple turns back and you haven't had to answer — and you still don't, yet.)

**Practical upshot, and it's good news for your merge:** this mostly *cancels the scrub*. If Manor stays = the Payor's GCP project, then the README and handbook prose ("the Manor has its own GCP project," "Manor's billing account," "RBRP — Manor identity") were **already correct** — no rename, no conflict churn on README.md et al. The watch-list I just built you largely dissolves. The remaining work is purely *additive*: introduce and home the **declared ledger** (in the Manor), which touches the paddock, not the Manor prose.

So the question back to you, to make sure I'm tracking your gut and not just re-pivoting to agree: is what you were protecting **"Manor stays a concrete, one-of GCP thing, and the ledger is a *new* object it hosts"** — with no grand polity invented unless multi-tenancy forces it? If yes, we keep Manor untouched and the only naming work left is the ledger itself.

aiTitle: Rename model and discuss citizen federation ideas

content.text:
Grounded in RBS0 now — and there's a clarifying find on step 1. Real state per step, then a proposed path.

**Step 1 — Manor is *already* a quoin; you're naming it, not minting it.** RBS0 catalogs the Payor project as `rbtgi_payor_project` → "RB Payor Project" (an "RB Google Instance"). Since we settled Manor = the Payor project, concrete and one-of, formal introduction is the lightest kind: give that existing quoin its cult display name (`<<rbtgi_payor_project, Manor>>`) and sweep the RBS0 references to "Payor Project" that should now read "Manor." No new anchor, no altitude collision — which is itself the confirmation our concrete decision was right. "All consequences" = that in-spec reference sweep, plus the README/handbook linked-term updates that now *add* Manor rather than rename a thing.

**Step 2 — the genuinely-new quoins, plus a category decision.** RBS0 has none of: Citizen, Capability-set, the Ledger, or the audit/drift family. Three things to settle in selection:
- **A category for the civic ontology.** RBS0 categorizes by prefix (`rbtr_` roles, `rbtgi_` GCP instances, `rbtgo_` operations). The citizen model is a new *civic ontology* and likely earns its own prefix (e.g. `rbtv_`, RB Term ciVic) to house citizen/capability-set/ledger/audit coherently rather than scattering them.
- **Citizen is not a role.** `rbtr_` means "RB Term Role" (director/retriever/governor). A Citizen is rank-neutral — rank lives in the capability-sets it holds — so it sits at the `at_operator` altitude (an identity that *holds* roles), not in `rbtr_`. That altitude call is a selection decision.
- **The Ledger's name — my lead candidate: Census.** Civic, etymologically bonded to *citizen* (civis→census), and the Roman census enrolled citizens *with their rank and holdings* — exactly "citizens with their capability-sets." Memorable, one word, distinct from generic "ledger." Alternate, if you want the grant-register flavor over the citizen-roll flavor: **Cartulary** (a register of charters/grants) — more precise, less memorable. (Capability-set itself probably also wants a cult name — **portfolio** or **office** are the civic fits — but you flagged the Ledger, so I'm leading there.)

**Step 3 — yes to a *functional* selection; no cult names yet; and they're coupled to retirements.** The paddock already fixes the function: **add-citizen / remove-citizen** (your "ledger membership"), **grant / revoke** (your "role affiliation"), plus **rekey** (keyfile-only) and **read/audit**. What's missing is their cult names — and minting them *retires* existing role-keyed `rbtgo_` verbs (`rbtgo_director_invest`, `…_divest`, `…_roster`, `rbtgo_governor_mantle`), since the paddock dissolves invest/divest/mantle/roster. The one real fork:
- **Membership** wants *civic* verbs — **enfranchise** / its inverse (disenfranchise, or the cleaner **expel** / **strike**-from-the-rolls).
- **Affiliation** — do we **repurpose invest/divest** (they literally mean "clothe with authority," which fits *capability* far better than the fused identity+capability meaning they're retired from), or mint fresh (feudal **enfeoff / disseise**, or plain **grant / revoke**)? Repurposing is semantically apt but echoes the retired meaning — a genuine call for you.

**Proposed path: select before incorporate.** Steps 2 and 3 are one minting pass — the verbs operate on the concepts, so name them together — and Manor's quoin naming rides along. Then incorporate *all* of it into RBS0 in one coherent write (new civic category + the concept quoins + the verb retirements + Manor's display name), so the spec never sits half-cross-referenced. Selection is finishable this chat; incorporation depends on how the naming lands.

Want me to draft the full candidate slate — the civic category prefix, the new concept quoins with names, and the verb set with the invest/divest fork worked both ways — for you to react to? Or react to the three seeds first (Census; the citizen-altitude call; enfranchise + the invest-repurpose question)?

content.text:
Good — Census/Citizen intended, and the split is clean: **Manor goes into the spec now** (it's current reality), **the civic ontology gets reserved in the heat** (intended, not built). Here's the verb opinion you asked for, then a ready-to-apply Manor proposal.

## Verbs — yes to low ceremony, but the gate isn't legacy, it's semantic fit

I agree we shouldn't ceremoniously retire-and-mint, and "no MVP yet" is real freedom — no deprecation dance, no flag day. But one refinement, because it changes the per-verb answer: **"no legacy" lowers the *cost* of either path; it doesn't change the *correctness test*.** A word that now means something other than its trained association is confusing whether or not there's shipped code — the legacy that actually bites is *conceptual* (your read, a future instance's read), and it lives in the very docs we're editing. So the gate is per-verb: **does the word's soul survive the new meaning?**

- **invest / divest → adjust in place.** The old meaning fused identity-creation + capability; the new meaning is the *capability half* — "clothe with authority." That's a clean narrowing, the soul survives. invest = grant a capability-set, divest = revoke. (They also collapse from role-keyed `rbtgo_director_invest` / `rbtgo_retriever_invest` into one generic `rbtgo_invest`.)
- **charter / knight → retire.** These are *role-bound* — "knight" *means* make-a-director. Forced role-generic, they'd lie. The word fights you.
- **roster → retire.** The concept itself dissolves (name-regex roster → reading the Census).
- **add/remove citizen → mint.** Genuinely new; no existing word fits. Civic: **enfranchise / expel** (or strike-from-the-rolls).
- **mantle** is the one judgment call — reflexive self-creation; probably becomes add-citizen + grant-governor-set, and may not survive as a primitive.

Net: adjust where the soul survives, retire where the word is role-bound or the concept is gone, mint where it's new. One structural note so "in place" isn't mistaken for zero-edit — even kept words change quoin *shape* (role-keyed → role-generic), so it's keep-the-word, collapse-the-quoins.

## Manor → RBS0, ready to apply

The good news from the read: the concept is **already defined** as `rbtgi_payor_project` (RBS0 line 1784) — "the administrative GCP project that hosts the Payor SA… control plane for managing depot projects." That's Manor exactly. So embracing it = make Manor the canonical name and enrich the definition with the property you kept losing — **its singularity**. Proposed definition:

```
[[rbtgi_manor]]
//axl_voices axig_project
{rbtgi_manor}::
The singular administrative {gcp_service} project that serves as an
installation's control plane — there is exactly one. It hosts the
{rbtr_payor} {giam_service_account}, the OAuth client, and the billing
account under which {rbtgi_depot} instances are created and funded.
Established by the {rbtr_payor} role.
```

Plus mapping-section entries: `:rbtgi_manor:` (display "Manor") and `:rbtgi_manor_p:` ("Manor's"), with `:rbtgi_payor_project:` kept as a resolving alias so nothing breaks.

**Sweep surface** (you wrote RBS0\*, so the whole family): the quoin is referenced in **RBSDE, RBSGS, RBSPE** (3 `{rbtgi_payor_project}` refs → flip to `{rbtgi_manor}`), and "payor project" appears in raw prose in **RBSAO, RBSPE, RBSPR, RBSPI, RBSRP** + RBS0:1063.

**The one scope call I need from you:** since Manor *is* the Payor project (same referent), do I convert the prose "payor project" → Manor **everywhere**, or keep "payor project" where the text means the literal GCP-resource/CRM-API target (e.g., RBSAO's "projects.get on the payor project") while Manor names the seat? My lean: convert to Manor throughout, phrasing as "the Manor / the Manor's project ID" where the resource mechanics matter — one name, no residual ambiguity.

## Civic ontology → reserved in the heat

To lock the intent without touching RBS0: I'll record the reserved quoins in the heat — **Census** (the ledger), **Citizen**, **capability-set** (name TBD — portfolio/office), and the verb dispositions above — as planned new quoins with a one-line gloss each, so the next mount has the world model without it being prematurely specced.

**Green light?** If you nod on the sweep scope, I'll apply the RBS0 Manor core + the 3 ref flips now, do the prose sweep next, and capture the reserved civic quoins in the paddock. Or if you'd rather refine the definition wording first, let's do that — it's the sentence meant to stop the circling, so it should be yours.

= Getting Started with Recipe Bottle

{at_recipe_bottle}
constructs container images under controlled conditions on trusted
{gcp_service}
infrastructure.
Developers retrieve these
{at_bottle_image_s}
and work within identical environments.
The
{rbtgi_repository}
serves as the single source of truth for container artifacts.

This guide explains the setup sequence and ongoing operational patterns.
Most of what follows happens once; the final sections describe day-to-day work.

== Core Concepts

{at_recipe_bottle} contains workloads safely.
A _vessel_ defines what to build--a specification for a container workload.
An {rbtga_ark} is the built result: the immutable artifact produced from that vessel.
Multiple {rbtga_ark_s} can exist for a single vessel, each with its own build timestamp and provenance.

Each {rbtga_ark} carries strong pedigree information including SBOMs and build metadata.
Organizations verify these attestations before deploying.

== Depots and Roles

A
{rbtgi_depot}
is the logical facility where container images are constructed and retained.
The
{rbtgi_repository}
within the {rbtgi_depot} stores all
{rbtgi_image_s}
available for retrieval.
Most organizations require exactly one {rbtgi_depot}.

{at_recipe_bottle}
provides bash command-line administration of {rbtgi_depot} lifecycle and operations across roles.

* *{rbtr_payor}*: Creates and funds {rbtgi_depot_s}; creates {rbtr_governor_s}. Billing flows through the {rbtr_payor_p} linked account. Manual OAuth setup enables all subsequent automation.
* *{rbtr_governor}*: Administers credentials within a {rbtgi_depot}. Creates {rbtr_director_s} and {rbtr_retriever_s} via service account authentication.
* *{rbtr_director}*: Submits builds, manages {rbtgi_image_s}.
* *{rbtr_retriever}*: Pulls {rbtgi_image_s} for local use.

The
{rbtr_payor}
stands apart--requires manual console work and OAuth authentication for both {rbtgi_depot} and {rbtr_governor} lifecycle operations.
The
{rbtr_governor}
and all downstream roles authenticate via
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
credentials, enabling automation without human interaction.

== Phase 1: Establish

{rbtgo_payor_establish}
is a guide for manual steps taken using the Google Cloud Console to create the
{rbtgi_payor_project},
link a billing account,
enable required APIs,
and configure an OAuth 2.0 client.

This phase produces a
{rbtgi_json_key_file}
containing OAuth client credentials.
This file must not be committed to version control.

== Phase 2: Setup

The administrator who completed establishment now executes automation from their
{at_workstation}.

{rbtgo_payor_install}
takes the
{rbtgi_json_key_file}
and completes the OAuth handshake.
A browser window opens for authorization.
Upon success, the
{rbro_refresh_token}
is stored in `~/.rbw/payor/rbro.env` with `600` permissions.
This file enables the
{rbtr_payor}
to authenticate for all future operations without repeating the browser flow.

{rbtgo_depot_levy}
provisions the {at_foundry} infrastructure.
Most installations require exactly one
{rbtgi_depot}-
a cohesive unit comprising a dedicated
{gcp_project_id},
a
{rbtgi_build_bucket},
a
{rbtgi_repository},
and the
{rbtr_mason}
service account that executes builds within the cloud.

The
{rbtr_payor}
can manage multiple {rbtgi_depot_s}:
{rbtgo_depot_list}
displays all active {rbtgi_depot_s} and their status;
{rbtgo_depot_unmake}
permanently removes a {rbtgi_depot} and all its resources.

The
{rbtr_payor}
then executes
{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
to create the
{rbtr_governor}
service account within the
{rbtgi_depot}.
This {rbtr_payor} operation produces an
{rbtgi_rbra_file},
stored at the path specified by
{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}.
The
{rbtr_governor}
can now operate independently of the
{rbtr_payor}.

== Credential Administration

The
{rbtr_governor}
manages the roster of credentialed users throughout the
{rbtgi_depot}
lifecycle.
While credential creation is typically infrequent, this administrative function remains active.

{rbtgo_director_invest}
provisions a
{rbtr_director}
service account, producing an
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
at the path specified by
{rbrr_director_rbra_file}.
Directors submit builds and manage images.

{rbtgo_retriever_invest}
provisions a
{rbtr_retriever}
service account, producing an
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
at the path specified by
{rbrr_retriever_rbra_file}.
Retrievers pull images for local use.

The
{rbtr_governor}
also maintains visibility into issued credentials through
{rbtgo_director_roster}
and
{rbtgo_retriever_roster},
and can revoke access through
{rbtgo_director_divest}
or
{rbtgo_retriever_divest}.

== Credential Files

Each {rbtr_role} maintains its own credential file.
Understanding these files helps administrators plan secure handling.

The
{rbtr_payor}
credentials reside in `~/.rbw/payor/rbro.env`, containing the
{rbro_client_secret}
and
{rbro_refresh_token}.
This file exists only on the administrator's
{at_workstation}.

The
{rbtr_governor},
{rbtr_director},
and
{rbtr_retriever}
each have an
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
at paths defined in the repository configuration.
Each file contains
{giam_service_account}
credentials scoped to one role within one
{rbtgi_depot}.

All credential files require `600` permissions, exclusion from version control, and secure distribution to authorized users.
A credential file cannot operate outside its designated role and {rbtgi_depot}.

== Day-to-Day Operations

Once setup and credential administration are complete, ongoing work proceeds through
{rbtr_director}
and
{rbtr_retriever}
roles.

=== Director Operations

The
{rbtr_director}
manages the build lifecycle.

{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
submits a container build to
{gcb_service}
via `builds.create`.
The
{rbtr_mason}
executes the build within the cloud and publishes the resulting
{rbtgi_image}
to the
{rbtgi_repository}.

{rbtgo_image_delete}
removes an
{rbtgi_image}
from the
{rbtgi_repository}.

**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.

=== Retriever Operations

The
{rbtr_retriever}
accesses built artifacts.

{rbtgo_image_retrieve}
pulls an
{rbtgi_image}
from the
{rbtgi_repository}
to the local
{at_workstation}.

{rbtgo_ark_inspect}
displays the trust posture of an
{rbtga_ark}
by querying GAR directly via the Registry API — no local summon required.
Authenticates as
{rbtr_retriever}.
Use {rbtc_plumb_compact} for a summary or {rbtc_plumb_full} for complete detail including package inventory and Dockerfile.
Hallmark-only invocation (single argument) navigates the hallmark subtree directly to locate the ark.

== Recovery

If `~/.rbw/payor/rbro.env` is lost, the administrator obtains a fresh
{rbtgi_json_key_file}
from Google Cloud Console and re-executes
{rbtgo_payor_install}.

If OAuth tokens expire,
{rbtgo_payor_refresh}
obtains fresh credentials.

If
{rbtr_governor}
credentials are compromised or need rotation, the
{rbtr_payor}
executes
{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
to replace the existing
{rbtr_governor}
service account with a fresh one, generating new
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
credentials.

To revoke a user's access, the
{rbtr_governor}
executes
{rbtgo_director_divest}
or
{rbtgo_retriever_divest}
for the target service account, invalidating the corresponding
{rbtgi_rbra_file}.

//axhob_operation
{rbtgo_payor_establish}

The
{at_operator}
performs one-time manual setup via Google Cloud Console to establish
{rbtr_payor}
OAuth infrastructure.
No API path exists for initial bootstrap - OAuth client creation requires human Console interaction.
This procedure creates the project and OAuth client but does NOT install credentials.

//axhos_step
. *Create Payor Project*:
.. Navigate to project creation in Console
.. Set project name: `Recipe Bottle Payor`
.. Set project ID: `rbw-payor` (preferred fixed name, single payor per account)
.. If `rbw-payor` already exists, use `rbw-payor-[4-digit-suffix]` (e.g., rbw-payor-2024)
.. {rbhg_store}
actual project ID as `«PAYOR_PROJECT_ID»` for subsequent operations
.. If Console displays "You need additional access" error after project creation, wait a few minutes and refresh the page.
GCP IAM changes are https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/access-change-propagation[eventually consistent] and may take time to propagate.

//axhos_step
. *Configure Billing*:
.. Access billing management in Console
.. Select or create billing account
.. Record
{rbrp_billing_account_id}
(format: XXXXXX-XXXXXX-XXXXXX)
.. Link billing account to payor project
.. {rbhg_require}
billing linkage for API operations

//axhos_step
. *Enable Required APIs*:
.. Navigate to APIs & Services > Enable APIs
.. Enable: Cloud Resource Manager, Cloud Billing, Service Usage, IAM, Artifact Registry
.. These enable programmatic depot management

//axhos_step
. *Configure OAuth Consent Screen*:
.. Navigate to APIs & Services > OAuth consent screen
.. Click "Get started" to launch Project Configuration wizard
.. Complete 4-step wizard:
... Step 1 - App Information: Set app name and user support email
... Step 2 - Audience: Select "External" (testing mode, no verification required)
... Step 3 - Contact Information: Provide email for notifications
... Step 4 - Finish: Accept User Data Policy and create

//axhos_step
. *Create OAuth 2.0 Client ID*:
.. Navigate to APIs & Services > Credentials
.. Create credentials > OAuth client ID
.. Application type: Desktop app
.. Name: "Recipe Bottle Payor"
.. Click CREATE
.. Popup displays client ID and client secret
.. Download JSON (filename: `client_secret_[id].apps.googleusercontent.com.json`)
.. {rbhg_warn}
Client secret visible only in this popup; June 2025 onwards, Google will not display it after closing

//axhoot_typed_output
{rbtgi_payor_project}
* Project ID (`«PAYOR_PROJECT_ID»`) recorded from step 1

//axhoot_typed_output
{oauth_token}
* OAuth client credentials: client ID and secret from the downloaded JSON file

//axhoc_completion
NOTE: Payor establishment creates the OAuth client configuration but does not perform credential installation.
Proceed to
{rbtgo_payor_install}
to complete setup by installing and testing OAuth authentication.

//axhob_operation
{rbtgo_payor_refresh}

Manual procedure to install OAuth credentials for new setup or refresh expired/compromised credentials.
Use this for both initial credential installation after
{rbtgo_payor_establish}
and for renewal operations.

//axhos_step
. *Obtain OAuth Credentials*:
.. For initial setup: Use JSON file downloaded during
{rbtgo_payor_establish}
.. For refresh/renewal: Navigate to APIs & Services > Credentials in Payor Project
... Click existing "Recipe Bottle Payor" OAuth client to open details
... If secret compromised:
.... Click "+ Add secret"
.... Click download icon next to NEW secret
.... Browser downloads `client_secret_[id].apps.googleusercontent.com.json`
.... Click "Disable" on secret with older creation date
.... Click trash icon to delete that disabled secret

//axhos_step
. *Install/Refresh Credentials*:
.. Run: `rbgp_payor_install ~/Downloads/client_secret_*.json`
.. This will:
... Guide you through OAuth authorization flow
... Store secure credentials in `~/.rbw/payor/rbro.env`
... Test the authentication
... Initialize depot tracking
... Update `RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` in the {rbrp_regime}

//axhog_guarantee
* `rbgp_depot_list` successfully displays current depots without authentication errors

//axhoc_completion
Procedure is complete when `rbgp_depot_list` successfully displays current depots without authentication errors.

NOTE: OAuth refresh tokens in testing mode expire after 6 months of non-use.

content.content: (mcp__vvx__jjx completed with no output)

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
else.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.

Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
  governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the declared ledger physically lives (a separate access-restricted object vs a GAR
  artifact) — explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director holds objectCreator there); the
  write-ACL must be held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant; see The audit).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent).
